---
title: "Exploring Simpson's paradox within the Penguin dataset"
subtitle: "and demosntrating the capabilities of Quardo"
title-block-banner: "#C3C7F9"
title-block-banner-color: "black"

author: 
 name: "Anastasiya Piyankova"
 affiliation: "HSE"
 orcid: "https://orcid.org/0000-0002-2633-2789"
 email: "amahotkina@gmail.com"

keywords: "Quardo, Spurious correlations, Simpson's paradox" 
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    toc-title: "Content"
    number-sections: true
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
editor: source
fig-cap-location: margin
---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## A few consideration about this doc
This Quarto document serves as a practical illustration of the concepts covered in the productive workflow online course. It's designed primarily for _educational purposes_, so the focus is on demonstrating Quarto techniques rather than on the rigor of its scientific content.
:::

## Introduction

This document offers a straightforward analysis of the well-known penguin dataset. You could read about this new **fantastic** dataset [here](https://allisonhorst.github.io/palmerpenguins/).

Let's load the libraries *now*!
 
```{r, message = FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Load packages
library(tidyverse)
library(patchwork)
library(tinytex)
library(DT)
library(knitr)
library(plotly)
library(hrbrthemes)
```
 
## Loading data and function

Here we are loading already cleaned data, that we previously processed. The original format was `exls`.
Let's load that clean version, with a few functions available in `functions.R`.

```{r, message = FALSE, warning=FALSE}
# Source functions
source(file = "functions.R")
# Read the clean dataset
data <- readRDS(file = "../input/clean_data.rds")
```

Note that `bill_length_mm` and `bill_depth_mm` have the following signification.

![Bill measurement explanation](asset/culmen_depth.png){width=300}

In case you’re wondering how the original dataset looks like, here is a searchable version of it, made using the `DT` package:

```{r, message = FALSE, warning=FALSE}
datatable(data, filter="top")
```

## Bill length and depth {#section-3}

Now, let's make some descriptive analysis, including **summary statistics** and **graphs**. 
What's striking is the  *slightly negative relationship* between `bill length`and`bill depth`:
 
```{r, message = FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.width = 5, fig.height = 5, fig.align='center'}
#Custom theme
AnastasiyaP_theme <- function() {
  theme_ipsum() +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_text(color = "#29266D", hjust=0.5, size = 16, face = "bold"),
      axis.text.x = element_text(size = 8),
      axis.text.y = element_text(size = 8),
      legend.position = "none",
      axis.line.x = element_line(size = 0.5, colour = "grey40", linetype=1),
      axis.line.y = element_line(size = 0.5, colour = "grey40", linetype=1),
      plot.margin = margin(20,20,30,20)
    )
}

p <- data %>%
  ggplot(
    aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm)
  ) +
  geom_point(color = "#69b3a2") +
  labs(
    x = "Bill length",
    y = "Bill depth",
    title = paste("Surprising relationship?")
  ) +
  AnastasiyaP_theme() 

ggplotly(p)
```

## Split your data by group 

Maybe it's **Simpson's paradox**^[Simpson’s paradox, or the Yule–Simpson effect, is a phenomenon in probability and statistics, in which a trend appears in several different groups of data but disappears or reverses when these groups are combined.read more [here](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Simpson%27s_paradox)]? Let's look at the plot by species.

```{r, message = FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.width = 5, fig.height = 5, fig.align='center'}
col_vec <- c(
  "Adelie" = "#FFAC31",
  "Chinstrap" = "#777CD3",
  "Gentoo" = "#343188"
)

pp <- data %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = bill_length_mm, y = bill_depth_mm, color = species)) +
  geom_point() +
  scale_color_manual(values = col_vec) +
  labs(
    x = "Bill length",
    y = "Bill depth",
    title = "There isn't surprising relationship"
  ) +
  AnastasiyaP_theme() 

ggplotly(pp)
```

It is also interesting to note that `bill length` and `bill depth` are quite different from one specie to another. 

The average of a variable can be computed as follow:
$${\displaystyle Avg={\frac {1}{n}}\sum _{i=1}^{n}a_{i}={\frac {a_{1}+a_{2}+\cdots +a_{n}}{n}}}$$
The average's of length and depth by species  are  summarized in the table below:

```{r}
bill_length_depth_mean <-  data %>%
  group_by(species) %>%
  summarise(mean_length = round(mean(bill_length_mm, na.rm = TRUE), 1),
            mean_depth = round(mean(bill_depth_mm, na.rm = TRUE), 1))

# Display in the Quarto report
kable(bill_length_depth_mean)

# Calculating specie's specific mean 
bill_length_adelie <- data %>%
  group_by(species) %>% 
  summarise(average_bill_length = mean(bill_length_mm, na.rm= TRUE)) %>% 
  filter(species == "Adelie") %>% 
  pull(average_bill_length) %>% 
  round(1)
```

For instance, the average bill length for the specie `Adelie` is `r bill_length_adelie`.

Now, let's check the relationship between bill depth and bill length for each  species separately. 

```{r, message = FALSE, warning=FALSE, fig.width = 9, fig.height = 3, fig.align='center', fig.cap="There is relationship between bill **length** and bill **depth**, when you split data by species."}

# Use the function in functions.R
p1 <- create_scatterplot(data, "Adelie", col_vec)+
  AnastasiyaP_theme()
p2 <- create_scatterplot(data, "Chinstrap", col_vec)+ 
  AnastasiyaP_theme()
p3 <- create_scatterplot(data, "Gentoo", col_vec)+ 
  AnastasiyaP_theme()

p1 + p2 + p3
```

## Conclusion

Thus we realize three distinct group of penguin. Within each of them we see positive correlation between `bill length` and `bill depth` unlike within among all of them simultaneously as shown in [section 3](#section-3)
